column1: # personal-token-denylist.tsv — STRUCTURAL/EXAMPLE rows only (temperloop#438,
# split out of the former operator-personal index; originally foundation #798,
# epic #762 kernel extraction). Checked by check-personal-token-denylist.sh
# against every path list-kernel-set.sh reports, and (diff-scoped) by
# check-pr-leak-guard.sh against a PR's added lines.
# THE SPLIT. This TRACKED, public kernel file ships only the MACHINERY and an
# illustrative example row. The operator-personal deny patterns (full name,
# personal emails, machine names, Sentry org slug, Obsidian vault path, the
# private GitHub org/handle) live in a sibling, GITIGNORED overlay file:
#     personal-token-denylist.local.tsv
# Both leak-guard checks UNION this tracked file with the .local overlay when
# the overlay is present, and DEGRADE LEGIBLY — a one-line NOTE on stderr,
# never a silent skip — when it is absent (a stranger's kernel-only clone).
# So a public clone enforces only the structural/example rows below; the
# operator's own checkout, which carries the untracked overlay, additionally
# enforces the personal rows. Shipping the personal ROWS in the public repo
# was a mild anti-pattern (no credentials, identity breadcrumbs only) — its
# MACHINERY is kernel-shippable and stays here. Historical blobs retain the
# pre-split rows (history is NOT rewritten); this is forward-going hygiene,
# not a flip blocker.
# To (re)create the overlay on an operator machine, recover the pre-split rows
# from git history and write them to the gitignored path:
#     git show <pre-split-commit>:workflows/scripts/kernel/personal-token-denylist.tsv \
#       > workflows/scripts/kernel/personal-token-denylist.local.tsv
# then edit to hold your own tokens. That path is gitignored; it can never be
# committed.
# Format: <pattern> TAB <description>
#   pattern     — an ERE (grep -E), matched case-sensitively against each
#                 kernel-set file's content. Keep patterns tight (word
#                 boundaries / anchors) to avoid false positives on
#                 unrelated substrings.
#   description — shown in the failure report; explains WHY the token is
#                 personal so a fix is obvious without re-deriving it here.
# A line legitimately needing one of these tokens (a structurally load-bearing
# default documented as an intentional exception — see check-kernel-manifest.sh
# analog "split" carve-out) may suppress a single line with a trailing
# `# denylist:allow — <reason>` comment; check-personal-token-denylist.sh skips
# any matched line carrying that marker. Use it SPARINGLY and only where the
# in-file rationale for the exception already exists (never to dodge a real
# scrub) — see check-personal-token-denylist.sh's own header for the two
# categories in use today (board.sh/funnel-tick.sh/funnel-drive.sh's
# boards.conf-fallback case maps, and their fixture-replay tests).
# Deliberately NOT covered here (see foundation #798 build's verification
# doc for the scoping rationale): the app/board names themselves
# (stageFind/foundation/ssmobile/subsetwiki) and their logical board numbers
# (3/4/5/6) — these are illustrative multi-board-pipeline examples, not
# identity/credential tokens, and a later item (plan-schema repo-field) is
# the one that replaces the hardcoded board-number mapping architecture.
# The single example below is INERT: the placeholder token never appears in
# real kernel content, so it matches nothing and enforces nothing. It exists
# only to document the row shape for anyone authoring the .local overlay, and
# to keep this file non-empty (both checks error on a zero-entry denylist).
# Put your REAL operator/personal deny patterns in the OVERLAY file, not here.
YOUR_ORG_OR_NAME_TOKEN_HERE
column2: EXAMPLE (structural, inert placeholder) — real operator/personal deny patterns belong in the gitignored personal-token-denylist.local.tsv overlay, never in this tracked file. See this file's header for the split.
